speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. We were talking about you working for the Winklers. What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1944. I worked for the Winklers. And the Winklers were-- I did my job and they were kind to me. I never had any nasty questions. There was no talk about nationality or anything. I did my job. And they weren't slave driving.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did you do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I polished parquetry floors. I helped cook. She taught me how to set table-- the table. And she didn't treat me as a servant. You know, she was nice to me. I did all the work and, I did-- I worked hard. I want to tell you one example that's important, sort of-- that throws back on Mrs. Moebius.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Winklers's household was just across the road-- excuse me-- not very far from Frau Moebius. And I had-- she used to leave the key under the mat in case she wasn't home and I had free running of the house, practically, at her place. So I used to go from Winklers's every day to the Moebiuses'. Why I'm telling you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I went across along the street during the day. I have acquired a little bit over-confidence in the meantime. And I told you everything is a connection. So has this, the P. And I walk along the street in [INAUDIBLE], a picturesque, small town. It had no industry apart of this-- of a cigar factory and a branch of 47 and 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I walked the street, who should I bump into? I don't bump into everybody. It was the chief of the police. Of course it was a small town, and he went straight on to me and he said, where is your P? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Where was it? I was warned not to wear it-- not to go out without it. Anyhow, he asked-- ordered me to make an appearance at the police station the next day. That's all I needed. I went into Frau Moebius's and I told her about it. And she said to me, in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN], you're not going by yourself. Well, that was a little bit of a consolation. When we came there, to the police station, I didn't realize her relationship with this police chief. She said to him, [SPEAKING GERMAN], Bruno? What do you want from the child, Bruno? He resented the familiarity. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The Moebiuses were Communists, known in town as Communists. He was-- had particular reason to resent that. And he started to convince her about the nobler German race and about some trouble they had in the vicinity of Poles, of [? Posen ?] with the Poles, that we were just-- Poles were just slave labor. And she heard all this and she said, I can give you a pledge here that she'll never venture out without her P. And as she said this, she dragged me by the hand and she says, it's all bullshit. I was horrified when I was outside. And she said to me, don't worry about this chap. I went to school with him. She said he has a cretinous child, whom the Germans, the Nazis, would take for experiments if I denounce him. He will never touch you, and you will never hear from him. And I never did. That was Frau Moebius. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Anyhow, I worked at Winklers's, continued to work at Winklers's at the time. You must know that 1944, on the 20th of July, there was an aborted assassination attempt on Hitler. At that time, there were seven million foreign workers in Germany, and they became more cautious [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and frightened of the foreign workers. The reason being that they had an emergency draft for boys over 15, I think, and for old men. And there were no men in the country. They were on the-- on the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they tried to flush out from unimportant centers, such as private household, the people to more important for them, more important-- something relevant to the war industry. What also happened, I told you that the Winklers were not Nazis. As a non-party members, they did not qualify for a domestic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Frau Winkler told me that herself. We are not party people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had-- every block in Germany was blessed-- and I'm saying this sarcastically-- with a house warden. House wardens were usually Nazis who acted as spies, more or less, to inform on the tenants. And we had a visit like this frequently, mostly under the pretext to watch whether it's fire safe, whether it's this.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, he came in. This chap was called [? Andreas ?] and he asked Frau Winkler, how long has this Pole-- this Polish girl been with you? And she said, 10 months. He scribbled 10 months, went through every room to pretend that it was fire security he had in mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few days-- it was before Christmas-- I got a message that my job has been canceled with the Winklers, and I have to report to a hotel in Hartha. It wasn't a very far away place. I can't remember, but it was about 20 or 30 kilometers from the town. I had to report to a hotel called [NON ENGLISH]. You would translate it as the white guardian. That was my farewell from the kind Winklers. And it was just-- I can't remember whether New Year's Day or day after that I arrived in Hartha. The place was called Hartha, spelled H-A-R-T-H-A, Hartha in Saxony. I just walk in through the front entrance, and I was shouted down by the owner that this was not entrance for bloody Polacks. They're not allowed to use the front entrance. Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he called the cook to introduce me to the routine. The routine was, again, that's why I'm so good at peeling potatoes and vegetables. And up to 11:00 or 12:00 or 1 o'clock, I don't know, till 2 o'clock. And then my task was to clean the rooms upstairs. It was all the guest rooms. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The owner of this place was called Pabst, P-A-B-S-T. Herr Pabst caught me at a crime. The light protruding from my door was-- I had stolen a newspaper from a wastebasket and reading it. Some guest left the newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came into the room-- which was fairly bear as it was-- removed the only bulb in the room and removed my blanket. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a problem [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- you could wash in the dark, but you couldn't comb long hair, long plaits, in the dark. So being an inventive brat, there was a guest's phone, telephone booth, and when you came into it, the light came on automatically. And it had some ad about pilsner beer. And you could-- and I did, for a few days, my hair in that phone booth-- in that phone booth till Mr. Pabst caught me again at this crime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was just about going to make good his word that he will punch me in the nose. Lucky for me, a very attractive woman came in and she didn't ask him, but he followed her with the eyes and he was more interested in her than he was in me. She came in. She parked her pram-- I didn't realize that this was fairly common practice there-- her baby pram downstairs. And she walked up the stairs to an officer's room and he followed her and this is how I-- and saved me from getting a beating. It was at that time that I sort of had a different perspective on the parade of baby prams. These women, they didn't know whether they widows. And there was a bit of promiscuity-- I don't think there were real tarts, but there was much promiscuity going on. And they disappeared into the officers' rooms. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyhow, I worked at the-- this hotel. Suddenly, one day, there were different noises. I looked out the little window, and there were Germans changing in the street into civilian clothes. The war was over, but nobody said so.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get up, and I see every door of every room open, and I ask someone brushing past me, where is Herr Pabst? And they said, how should I know? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the end of the Third Reich-- not officially yet-- and it was an end to a thousand-year dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- was about the 6th of May, 1945. I'm telling you about two days before the capitulation. I was fighting with my thoughts, one minute hoping, one minute despairing. I had nowhere to go. And I joined the pilgrimage, the procession of people, because in the streets, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just two days after the-- or a day after the capitulation-- no transport. This was occupied by the Russians, that part, and people were rushing on foot, trying to get to the Western zone to the Americans. I had no [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will to leave, to-- to leave there because I thought that the few times I tried to dispatch a fairly camouflaged car to Poland that someone might be looking for me just there. So in the morning-- so I went back to the Moebiuses. When I came back, Frau Moebius looked very distraught. And it was only after I found her behavior strange that she told me her husband had got home the last week. He got himself home, and he suffered a heart attack. She just got me onto his bed. [CRYING] And she said, I told you she would come if they let her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that night, I lost a dear German friend. [CRYING] It might sound strange, to what's happened to my family, to say that, but I'm here. And he was a friend. And I attended his funeral. And the most unorthodox things that came to my mind, standing above that plot allotted to Paul Moebius was that my people didn't have a decent funeral. They have no names-- and no tombs-- no marked names and no tombstones. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was left totally alone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what else you want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Lwow after?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never wanted to see Lwow, but I did not expect-- somehow, secretly in my mind, I thought, well after all, I survived. Someone must have. But it was childish, naive expectation. No one survived. I stayed in Germany from morning to night. At the beginning, there were very intensive search lists on the radio, people trying to find their dear ones. And Frau Moebius, say, from A to C, I would watch it, and I would give a list of names from-- from D to F or so. And she-- because I had to run out to the toilet, I didn't want to miss. She was very unlucky. Because her son did not return, her only son. She was an old lady, and I left for Berlin. Actually, she gave me an address. It was difficult to stay, for a Jew or for a foreigner, in the Russian zone because the Russians started to send the people to their countries that they belonged to. I enrolled in a course, thinking, when I emigrate, I would want to be someone or to do something. And in the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I told her that. And she gave me her address, the address of her sister in Friedenau, in Berlin, which was an American zone. And I went from there to Berlin on a train. Nobody could recognize, sort of no one was looking at a young girl who spoke good German. Her sister's name was Smith. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stayed there for a while, but I had to fend for myself. I found myself a job [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] as a translator of films. They were educational films. And the fellow who ran this [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] place was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- what sort of identity did you have? Jewish identity at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I then-- I then-- yes, I then had the Jewish identity. It's strange, you know, how you got away all the time with being a Pole. And one day they told me, when I was in Berlin, that a lot of people came from other camps. But Berlin, at the time, it was not-- there were no facilities, no communication. People used to leave little slips under a stone where they were. And I walked towards Schlachtensee because they told me that a lot of Jews came to Schlachtensee from other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ironically, a German lady joined me. She took one look at me. I was so proud of my acting skills, pretending I was a Pole. She took one look at me and she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], haven't you people had it hard during the war? Didn't you people have it hard during the war? She took one look at me. You see, where-- where I was, in this small place, nobody sort of-- they didn't have any foreigners there. And nobody suspected me of being Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, to make it short, I reported to-- to a Jewish organization and at that time there was no planes between Berlin-- you couldn't get out of Berlin. Only what they called the air bridge, luftbrucke. And one day they notified me they had room for me on that luftbrucke. I went to West Germany, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] enrolled in a couple of courses there, and then I met my husband. In 1950, we came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back, where-- how did you find out about your family? What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find out. Everybody said there's no trace of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your husband? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in '49, could have been '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in Munich. And we came to Australia from Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because it's so far from Europe. And I wanted to get away. Actually, I had made some contact with relatives in America, but they were sort of procrastinating. I don't think they wanted me very much. And it was very hard to get to America unless somebody guaranteed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for you. They were different people, you know, like the people here. And these people, they were relatives legally, but they've never met me. I was born long after they left for the States. Had I persevered, perhaps I would have got away. But I met my husband and we came to Australia in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came by-- by boat called

speaker: interviewer
text: And which city did you arrive in in

speaker: interviewee
text: Australia? We arrived in-- originally in Sydney and then we went to Brisbane by some-- someone read the passenger list, and they thought that Brisbane would be easier for jobs. But as it appears, it wasn't. Though I secured a good job in Brisbane, I trained-- I made a crash course, kind of [INAUDIBLE] 12 months course in-- in Munich as a-- as a lab technician. Because we were looking for skills that you could utilize as a migrant. And I got myself a job at the Brisbane blood bank. And I have a photo there which will tell you that I was promoted from this lab job, staring into a microscope, counting-- doing blood counts, to a PR job. They tried and-- you see, in Europe, blood is not donated. It's being paid for. And they tried to move migrants to tell them, give them a message, that they are recipients of blood, but they're not donors. And it was my task to do this. And from the photo that you have there, which I left with you, you'll find out that it was successful because we had queues of migrants. I was picked up by a doctor who looked like the migrant-- I don't remember the camp, but it was like a Victorian Bonegilla migrant. And I spoke to these people in German and they came in droves to give their blood. And then we moved to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find it difficult when you first arrived in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, difficult is a sort of-- it's quite a loose term. It's like I heard a term the other day about poverty, poverty in India and poverty that your fridge isn't big enough. We had no social help. We're not on a welfare. But we are both working. We are grateful of the freedom. It's the best country in the world. And it was like a life kind of nectar for people, for survivors, who come here and complain-- we ought to send them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you come to Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Australia in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about 18 months later, we moved from Brisbane to Melbourne. We've been here since, and I have managed to get an Australian education in the meantime. Made my life the best I could, but none of my family survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kahane, thank you very much. That's the end of tape four.
